prompt: A child's hand-stitched patchwork quilt filling the entire frame from edge to edge, photographed flat from directly above like a birds eye view. The quilt is made of many small fabric squares stitched together with visible thread. Most squares are whole, warm, and colourful — soft faded patterns of small stars, tiny flowers, little animals, stripes, gentle plaids in muted yellows, dusty blues, faded greens, worn pinks. Clearly handmade, clearly loved, clearly old. Every fifth square is destroyed. Burnt black and charred at the edges, the fabric eaten away, some reduced to just the stitching outline with nothing inside — a ghost square, a hole where warmth used to be. The burning is not dramatic or fiery. It is cold and already done. The destruction is quiet and systematic and repeating across the entire quilt. The pattern of destruction — one in every five — is visible and countable across the whole surface. The quilt fills the complete frame, no background visible, no floor, no hands, no people. Just the fabric. Soft natural light from one side casting gentle shadows in the stitching. Macro photography, extreme detail in the fabric texture, thread, weave, and char. Muted warm tones overall, the burnt squares cold and grey-black against the warmth.
dataset_name: Stop the War on Children – Children in Conflict Data
insight: In 2024, one in five children globally — more than 500 million — lived in a conflict zone, the highest share ever recorded.
